en_body:
10/1992, Hợp-Lưu magazine made its debut before its readers. As editor Khánh-Trường confided at length, "Hợp-Lưu was born within circumstances entirely unfavorable to the journal's principles and purpose: calling for cooperation among literary writers inside and outside the country...Hợp-Lưu has long been a playground for writers belonging to different spaces, times and tendencies. And it will continue to be so."

 Trần Vu, author of "The House Behind the Temple of Literature": "Everything began with a letter. (Khánh-Trường) called for renewal in Vietnamese literary life overseas. The letter abandoned the attitude of crossing the river then destroying the bridge, the blockade of so many years. The letter acknowledged the existence of the Vietnamese person after 1975 upon the homeland, even if that person was not Nationalist, not Republican...He (Khánh-Trường) drew back to himself so many young writers of that time: Trân Sa, Lê Thị Thấm Vân, Vu Quỳnh Nh., Nguyên Thị Thanh-Bình, Phạm Thị Ngọc, Phạm Chi Lan, Nguyên Thị Ngọc Lan, Nguyên Hoàng Nam, Hoàng Mai Đạt, Đô Kh, Trầm Phục Khắc, Ngọc Khôi, Hồ Đình Liêm, Vu Đình Kh., Vinh Hảo.

 In the years 1978-1979, though there were no computers, no internet, no Vietnamese fonts, everything still copied by hand, typed then accented by hand, books displayed for sale beside food; writers and readers scattered, hardship everywhere. Yet Vo Phiến set forth with the immeasurable fierce homesickness of an entire class of displaced people. Every Vietnamese in exile cherished Vietnamese letters, loved, embraced and preserved them, those Quốc-Ngư letters of the multitude so unexpectedly defeated.

 1982, Mai Thảo determinedly republished Văn magazine, as overseas literary activity began to warm. Mai Thảo, leader of Southern arts and letters, easily gathered his fellow writers of the same time: Con Hùm Xám Vu Khắc Khoan, Thanh Nam, Mặc Đô, Nghiêm Xuân Hồng, Túy Hồng, Viên Linh, Nguyên Đông Ngạc, Trang Chau, Tạ Tỵ, Trần Ngọc Minh, Hà Huyền Chi. Mai Thảo also discovered, recognized, and encouraged women writers: Lê Thị Huệ, Phan Thị Trọng Tuyến, Nguyên Thị Ngọc Nhung, Trần Diệu Hằng, Cao Bình Minh. At the same time, each month, the column "Young Writers" introduced a new face to the literary village; sometimes one issue held as many as four new writers: Nguyên Thạch Giang, Ý Ngôn, Vu Phương Nam, Đặng Mai Lan.

 Then Làng Văn magazine entered the village. Làng Văn inherited "the atmosphere blazing with momentum" of a community growing toward full vigorous ripeness, together with the burning homesickness of the newly arrived boat people. A new magazine, a new generation of readers with a new sentiment: Huyền Châu, Tuệ Nga, Hồ Trường An, Ngô Nguyên Dung, Nguyên Ngọc Ngạn, Lê Văn Lân, Ngọc Khôi, Nguyên Tấn Hưng, Thái Văn Kiểm, Vo Kỳ Điền, Nguyên Văn Ba, Trần Long Hồ, Phan Ni Tấn, Luân Hoán, Lâm Hảo Dung, Diệu Tần, Hoàng Xuân Sơn, Nguyên Đức Bạt Ngàn, etc...

 1985, Vo Phiến republished "Văn Học Nghệ Thuật" magazine, renaming it "Văn-Học," administered by Nguyên Mộng Giác: it may be considered the most prestigious magazine of that literary era. The magazine's pillar contributors included: Nhật-Tiến, Vo Đình, Lê Tất Điều, Định Nguyên, Tưởng Năng Tiến, Vo Phiến, Nguyên Bá Trạc, Hà Thúc Sinh, Nguyên Tất Nhiên, Thế Uyên, Hoàng Khởi Phong, Trần Hoài Thư, Hoàng Ngọc Tuấn, Nguyên Xuân Hoàng. There were also new pens: Khế Iêm, Ngu Yên, Phạm Việt Cường, Phan Tấn Hải, Kiệt Tấn, Thường Quán, Cao Xuân Huy, Trịnh Y Thư, Nguyên Đức Lập, Nguyên Ý Thuần, Hồ Đình Nghiêm, Nguyên Thị Hoàng Bắc, Nguyên Manh Trinh, Trần Trúc Giang, Trần Thị Kim Lan, Trần Thị Lai Hồng, Trân Sa, Lê Bi, Vu Huy Quang, Trương Vu, Thế Giang, Trần Vu, Đô Kh., Mai Kim Ngọc, Vu Quỳnh Hương, Nhược Thủy (Y Chi).

 Because the magazines above gathered so many veteran writers of the literary villages at home and overseas, I have recorded their pen names for readers inside and outside the country to research. I hope you will have meaningful experiences while strolling through the Vietnamese literary village after...communism *-()
note: The title is a Vietnamese proverb meaning that every undertaking is hardest at its beginning.
